segments.s043-g41: برین گونه is an archaic/poetic spelling of بر این گونه = 'in this manner' (بر + این + گونه), placed before the rest as an adverbial phrase. از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place/seat' (از = from, جای = place/position). برخاستند is the simple past (3rd person plural) of برخاستن, meaning 'they rose' or 'they stood up'; here it completes the clause 'they rose from their places.' Word order is typical Persian: adverbial/manner → place phrase → verb.
segments.s043-g42: همه شب is a temporal adverbial meaning 'the whole night' or 'all night long.' همی is an archaic narrative particle here giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept on' / 'was continually'), commonly found before past verbs in epic style. چاره is the object meaning 'a remedy/expedient, i.e. a plan or scheme,' and آراستند is past (3rd pl.) of آراستن meaning 'they arranged/ prepared/put in order,' so the phrase means 'they spent the whole night arranging a plan.'
segments.s043-g17: Que/relative word + copula introduce an interrogative/exclamatory clause: 'که هست' here functions like 'what/which is'. 'این سزاوار شاهنشهی' is a nominal predicate: 'این' (= this) is the topic, 'سزاوار' (= worthy/fit) is the adjective/predicate and 'شاهنشهی' (= kingship/imperial dignity) is the complement introduced directly after it (Persian often places qualifiers after the noun they modify). The whole segment reads: 'What is this that is fit for imperial kingship?'.
segments.s043-g18: This is a short verbless/pro-drop clause typical in poetry where the object-marked phrase 'جز این را' ('except this' with 'را' marking it as definite object) is followed by the verb 'نزیبد' (a present form of 'زیبیدن' used here in the sense 'befits'). Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: subject/agent ('کلاه مهی' = 'the moon‑like crown') follows the verb; literal structure: 'Except this (جز این را), [does] befit — (is) the moon‑like crown.' In natural English: 'Nothing except this befits it: the moon‑like crown.'
segments.s043-g19: Light‑verb construction: the noun 'نگه' (gaze/watch) combines with the light verb 'کرد' to form 'نگه کرد' = 'looked'. Word order is verb + (proper) name: 'به لشکر' ('toward the army') is the prepositional complement, then the verbal phrase, and finally the subject/name 'سلم' follows as is common in older/poetic Persian. 'از کران' ('from the edge') is a locative adjunct.
segments.s043-g20: 'سرش گشت' is a small clause where 'سر' ('head', here used as 'mind/feeling') takes the possessive enclitic 'ش' ('his') and the past verb 'گشت' ('became/turned'); together 'his head/mind turned' = 'his mind was affected/turned away'. 'از کار لشکر گران' is a causal/agentive phrase introduced by 'از' (here 'because of'/'by') with 'کار لشکر گران' = 'the work/affair of the heavy/powerful army' explaining the cause or domain. In short: 'his mind was upset/overwhelmed because of the heavy work of the host.'
segments.s043-g21: Simple movement clause: 'به لشگرگه آمد' = 'he came to the camp'. The following nominal phrase 'دلی پر ز کین' ('a heart full of kīn') functions as an adverbial describing his state on arrival: 'with a heart full of hatred/vengeance'. 'پر ز' is a poetic sequence meaning 'full of' followed by the source/contents introduced by 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از').
segments.s043-g22: Two parallel nominal images linked by parataxis (no conjunction): 'جگر پر ز خون' = 'the heart/guts full of blood' (poetic use of 'جگر' as seat of feeling), and 'ابروان پر ز چین' = 'eyebrows full of wrinkles/lines' (i.e. furrowed brows). Repetition of 'پر ز' creates a stacked descriptive effect typical in epic poetry.
segments.s043-g23: 'سراپرده پرداخت' is a poetic verb phrase meaning 'he withdrew/withdrew to the inner curtain/inner chamber' — 'پرداخت' here is used in an intransitive, archaic/poetic sense 'proceeded/withdrew' (not just 'pay'). 'از انجمن' ('from the assembly') states the origin/scene he left. The sequence shows motion away from the public meeting toward a private space.
segments.s043-g24: Coordination 'خود و تور بنشست' = 'he himself and Tur sat down' (subject expressed as two coordinated items). 'با رای زن' is an instrumental/ablative phrase introduced by 'با' meaning 'with' or 'in accordance with' and modifies the sitting: they sat following/using 'the woman's counsel/opinion' (رای = counsel, زن = the woman), i.e. 'in line with the woman's plan.'
segments.s043-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تنگ here is the person/agent coming (treated as a name or noun); اندر آمد is the past tense compound verb 'came in / came among' (اندر = 'into/among', آمد = past 'came'). به نزدیکشان is a prepositional phrase 'to/near them', with the pronominal suffix -شان attached to نزدیک.
segments.s043-g2: نبود is the past negative/existential 'there was not / was not' (implying lack of awareness). آگه 'aware' is the predicate that follows نبود. از introduces the domain 'of/regarding', linking آگه to رای تاریکشان = 'their dark counsel/plan'.
segments.s043-g3: پذیره شدندش is a verbal phrase meaning 'they received/accepted him'; here پذیره functions as the verbal/adjectival stem and شدند (past plural) completes it, with the clitic -ش = 'him' attached to شدند. به آیین خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in/by their custom' (آیین = custom/practice, خویش = 'their own' formed from خوی + ش).
segments.s043-g4: سپه سربسر باز بردند پیش is a clause 'they again brought the whole army before (him)'. سربسر is an adverbial 'entirely/completely' modifying بردند (past plural 'they carried/led'), باز = 'again', and پیش = 'before/in the presence of'. Word order places the object and adverbial elements before the verb, typical in older Persian.
segments.s043-g5: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. دیدند is past plural 'they saw'. روی برادر is the noun phrase 'the brother's face' (روی = face, followed by برادر). به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with affection / in kindness' where به + noun expresses manner.
segments.s043-g6: یکی = 'one (of them)'. تازه‌تر is the comparative 'fresher / more renewed' (تازه + comparative -تر). برگشادند is a compound past verb 'they opened/revealed/brightened' (بر- prefix + گشادند past plural), and چهر 'face/countenance' is its object; together it means 'one of them showed a somewhat fresher countenance.'
segments.s043-g7: دو پرخاشجوی = 'two quarrelsome (men)' (دو = 'two', پرخاشجوی = 'quarrelsome/aggressive'). با یکی نیک خوی = 'with one of good disposition' where با = 'with', یکی = 'one / someone (of them)', نیک = 'good', and خوی = 'disposition/temper'.
segments.s043-g8: گرفتند is past plural 'they took/posed' and here means 'they put/asked' (a question). پرسش = 'question'. نه is the negation 'not', and بر آرزوی = 'about/regarding a wish/request' where بر functions like 'concerning' and آرزوی = 'wish'; together نه بر آرزوی means 'not concerning a petition.'
segments.s043-g25: سخن شد is an intransitive clause meaning ‘a talk/speech happened/began’; پژوهنده here modifies سخن (a probing/inquisitive talk). از هردری is a prepositional phrase ‘from/at every door’ (poetic for ‘from every quarter’). Word order is inverted for poetical effect: predicate (سخن شد) + modifier (پژوهنده) + source phrase (از هردری).
segments.s043-g26: ز شاهی و از تاج هر کشوری are two coordinated prepositional phrases giving the topics of the talk: ز/از function as poetic prepositions meaning ‘about/ concerning’ or ‘from’ and link to شاهی (kingship) and تاج هر کشوری (the crown of every realm). The coordination with و ties the two topics together.
segments.s043-g27: به تور marks the indirect object/recipient (‘to Tur’). از میان سخن is an adverbial phrase ‘from/out of the midst of the talk’. سلم گفت is the core clause ‘Salm said’ (Salm is the speaker); the order places the recipient and adverbial before the subject and verb for narrative pacing.
segments.s043-g28: که introduces the content clause (‘that/why ...’). یک‌یک سپاه is the subject phrase ‘each army’ (one by one/each). از چه گشتند is a question-style indirect clause ‘from/for what (reason) did they become…’ and جفت is the predicate noun ‘paired’ placed at the end; گشتند is the past plural verb ‘they became’. Overall: ‘that each army for what reason became paired.’
segments.s043-g29: به هنگامه بازگشتن ز راه is a temporal adverbial: به هنگامه ‘at the time/at the moment’ + بازگشتن (verbal noun ‘returning’) + ز راه ‘from the road’. The verbal noun بازگشتن functions as the noun governed by هنگامه.
segments.s043-g30: نکردی is the past negative (‘you did not do’); here it combines with the noun نگاه to mean ‘did not look/ did not cast a gaze’. همانا is an emphatic particle ‘truly/indeed’. The phrase order places the noun (نگاه) after the verb (an inversion common in poetic narration) and به لشکر marks the target of the look (‘toward the army’).
segments.s043-g31: سپاه دو شاه is the subject ‘the army/armies of the two kings’. از پذیره شدن uses از + verbal noun (پذیره شدن ‘being received/accepted’) to express a respect or regard (‘with regard to being received’). شدن here is the auxiliary/verbal noun-forming element.
segments.s043-g32: دگر بود means ‘were different’ (دگر = other/different, بود = was/were). و دیگر به بازآمدن repeats the contrast: ‘and (were) different again with respect to returning’ — دیگر here marks the second point of difference and بازآمدن is the verbal noun ‘returning’. The clause states two differing aspects of the armies.
segments.s043-g9: دو دل پر ز کینه is a compact nominative phrase: دو (the numeral) precedes دل (noun) to mean 'two hearts'; پر ز + noun is a poetic way to say 'full of X' where ز = 'of/from'. یکی دل به جای is an appositive/contrast: یکی modifies دل ('one heart') and به جای ('in its place') is a prepositional phrase marking that one heart remained 'in place'. Note the inverted, elliptical poetic ordering.
segments.s043-g10: برفتند is the past-tense plural verb 'they went/departed' and comes before the explicit subject here for poetic emphasis; هر سه ('the three/all three') names the participants and functions as the subject, while به پرده سرای is the goal phrase introduced by به ('to') meaning 'to the curtained chamber' (پرده = curtain). The final word سرای in the manuscript is used as a locative 'house/room' (sense not listed below).
segments.s043-g11: به ایرج is a prepositional phrase 'toward Irāj' marking the target of the look; نگه کرد is a light-verb construction ('made a look' = 'looked'), where نگه (gaze) + کرد (did) yields 'looked'. یکسر modifies سپاه ('the whole army') and appears after the verb in this line — a common poetic inversion placing the verb early for emphasis.
segments.s043-g12: که introduces an explanatory/causal subordinate clause ('for/because'); او is the subject ('he'); بد here functions adverbially/adjectivally with سزاوار to form 'badly fit' or 'ill-suited' (بد سزاوار = 'not fitting'); تخت و کلاه are the two nouns coordinated by و ('throne and crown'), the things for which he is said to be unfit.
segments.s043-g13: بی‌آرامشان is an adjectival phrase 'their unrest/restlessness' (بی‌آرام + possessive suffix -شان). شد is the past copula 'became', and دل is the logical subject 'the heart'; از مهر او 'from/of his love' gives the cause. The line uses inversion (possessed adjective phrase before شد and دل) typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s043-g14: This is a coordination of two parallel nominal phrases with an omitted verb (ellipsis of 'شد' repeated): دل از مهر (the heart because of love) and دیده پر از چهر او (the eyes full of his face). Note the pattern: noun + adjective/quantifier (دیده پر) + از phrase to indicate content ('full of ...'). The conjunction و links the two halves.
segments.s043-g15: سپاه پراگنده شد is the main clause: سپاه (the army) + predicate پراگنده شد ('became scattered' / 'dispersed'). جفت جفت is a reduplicated adverbial 'pair by pair' or 'two by two', placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s043-g16: همه is the subject 'everyone/every one'; نام here functions as 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame, so نام ایرج بد is 'Irāj's bad name' or 'the ill repute of Irāj' (the adjective بد qualifies نام). اندر نهفت is the verb phrase 'hid within' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hid/concealed'), with the object placed before the verb as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s043-g33: که begins a subordinate clause ('that/when/whereas') introducing the circumstance. چندان + کجا works as an adverbial phrase meaning 'to such an extent / however far/wherever', and راه بگذاشتند is a past-tense verb phrase: گذاشتند here means 'they set out on / they went along (the) road' (literal 'they left/put the road'). Word order is typical of classical Persian: adverbial + place + verb.
segments.s043-g34: یکی ... نه is an idiomatic negative indefinite: 'not one'. چشم از ... برداشتند is a fixed structure meaning 'to take one's gaze/eyes from X' — here نه (negation) precedes the verb برداشتند (past plural), so the clause says they did not remove their eyes from Iraj.
segments.s043-g35: از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'for/from Iran'. دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م). خود here intensifies the possessor ('my very/own heart'). به دو نیم بود is a past-state: بود 'was' and به دو نیم 'into two halves / split in two'.
segments.s043-g36: به introduces the domain ('in/among/to') and governs اندیشه اندیشگان 'the thought/attention of the thoughtful' (اندیشه = thought; اندیشگان = the thoughtful/thinkers). برفزود is a third-person past of فزودن with the prefix بر- meaning 'he increased/aroused/added', so the clause means he caused an increase in thought among the sensible/wise.
segments.s043-g37: سپاه دو کشور is a genitive phrase 'the armies of the two countries/realms'. چو here = 'when', and کردم is first-person past 'I made/did' used with نگاه 'gaze' to mean 'when I turned/let fall my gaze'. The verb is in the speaker's past ('I looked').
segments.s043-g38: از این پس is an adverbial 'from this on/after this'. جز او را is 'except him' with the object marker را. نخوانند is a present/subjunctive classical form of خواندن used here in a general/future sense 'they will call/name', and شاه is the predicative noun 'king' governed by that verb ('they will call/declare none king except him').
segments.s043-g39: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. بیخ او 'his root' is the object, and نگسلانی is an archaic subjunctive/imperative form with the negative prefix ن- + گسلانی (from گسلاندن) meaning 'do not loosen/unroot'. ز جای 'from the place' completes the idea 'if you do not unroot his root from its place'.
segments.s043-g40: ز تخت بلندت 'from your lofty throne' has ز as 'from', تخت 'throne', and بلندت = بلند + -ت (your) 'your lofty'. کشد is third-person singular present/future 'he will pull/drag' (classical present used for future), and زیر پای 'under the foot / underfoot' expresses being cast down beneath the feet. The clause says he will drag (you) from your high throne and trample you.

sentences.s043-s21: In this way they rose from their places; all night long they busied themselves arranging a plan.
sentences.s043-s9: What is fit for imperial kingship? Nothing but this — a moon‑like crown befits it.
sentences.s043-s10: Salm looked toward the host from the edge; his mind/ head was affected by the heavy toil of the army.
sentences.s043-s11: He came to the army‑camp with a heart full of hatred; a heart full of blood, eyebrows furrowed with lines.
sentences.s043-s12: He withdrew behind the inner curtain from the assembly, and he and Tur sat down according to the woman's counsel.
sentences.s043-s1: When Tang came close to them, he was unaware of their dark counsel.
sentences.s043-s2: They received him according to their custom, and again led the entire army before him.
sentences.s043-s3: When they saw their brother's face with affection, one of them brightened his countenance more.
sentences.s043-s4: The two quarrelsome men, together with one of good temper, asked a question—not concerning a petition.
sentences.s043-s13: A probing discussion arose at every doorway about kingship and about the crown of every realm.
sentences.s043-s14: Addressing Tur from the midst of the conversation, Salm said why each army had become paired.
sentences.s043-s15: At the moment of returning from the road, truly you did not cast a glance toward the army.
sentences.s043-s16: The armies of the two kings differed in being received, and differed again with respect to returning.
sentences.s043-s5: Two hearts full of hatred, one heart remaining in its place — the three of them went to the curtained chamber.
sentences.s043-s6: The whole army turned its gaze to Irāj, for he was ill-suited to the throne and crown.
sentences.s043-s7: Their hearts became restless from his love; the heart was full of love and the eyes full of his face.
sentences.s043-s8: The army broke up, pair by pair; each hid Irāj's ill repute within (his heart).
sentences.s043-s17: However far they went along the road, not one of them took his eyes off Iraj.
sentences.s043-s18: My heart for Iran itself was split in two; he stirred thought among the thoughtful.
sentences.s043-s19: When I cast my gaze over the armies of the two realms, from that time on they will call no one king except him.
sentences.s043-s20: If you do not unroot him from his place, he will drag (you) from your high throne underfoot.
